Title: A Case Study in Migrating Customer Support Issue Classification Models to BERT-based Architecture

The article discusses a case study conducted by Picnic, a company that provides customer support services, on migrating their existing issue classification models to a new architecture based on the BERT model. The old architecture used logistic regression and required separate models for every use case and geography, leading to high maintenance costs. 

The new architecture uses the distilbert-base-multilingual-cased model from HuggingFace, which is trained using a unified dataset of messages annotated by human experts. The new model significantly outperformed the old one, with f-beta=2 scores improving by more than 10%. Picnic was able to roll out the new models across all case origins and markets in just eight weeks, reducing maintenance costs and addressing the challenges posed by the old architecture. 

The article also mentions that Picnic is considering using large language models (LLMs) for future customer support operations, but this will be explored in a future blog post.

Summary:
- The article discusses a case study conducted by Picnic on migrating their issue classification models to a new BERT-based architecture. 
- The old architecture used logistic regression and required separate models for every use case and geography, leading to high maintenance costs. 
- The new architecture uses the distilbert-base-multilingual-cased model from HuggingFace, which significantly outperformed the old one. 
- Picnic was able to roll out the new models in eight weeks, reducing maintenance costs and addressing the challenges posed by the old architecture. 
- The article mentions that Picnic is considering using large language models (LLMs) for future customer support operations.

Analysis:
- The article provides a detailed case study on migrating issue classification models to a new BERT-based architecture, which significantly outperformed the old logistic regression-based models. 
- The new architecture uses a unified dataset of messages annotated by human experts and reduces maintenance costs by using one global model per use case. 
- Picnic was able to roll out the new models in eight weeks, addressing the challenges posed by the old architecture. 
- The article mentions that Picnic is considering using large language models (LLMs) for future customer support operations.

Keywords:

{
"article_title": "Improving Customer Service with Precog: A Deep Dive into Nubank's AI-Powered Routing System",
"article_summary": "This article discusses Nubank's Precog, an AI-powered routing system that improves customer service by accurately predicting the product a customer needs help with. The system uses contrastive learning to learn embeddings of events in a self-supervised manner and has resulted in significant decreases in time to solve issues and increases in customer satisfaction.",
"keywords": ["Nubank", "Precog", "AI", "customer service", "routing system", "contrastive learning", "embeddings", "self-supervised learning"],
"article_body": "In this article, we delve into Nubank's Precog, an AI-powered routing system that has revolutionized the way customer service is handled at the company. The system uses advanced machine learning techniques to predict the product a customer needs help with, thereby improving efficiency and customer satisfaction. 

Precog works by encoding events (e.g., app click stream, transactions) as a sequence of symbols. This allows the use of techniques originally developed for natural language processing, like embeddings and sequence models, to understand and predict customer needs. The richest source of data that was missing from Nubank's models was their app click stream, so that's where Precog started. 

The main challenge with this data is its lack of enforced structure across different product teams. Each record is identified by a metric name and a JSON with attribute/value pairs, all of which are determined by the engineers who implement each flow. In order to avoid development bottlenecks, there is no centralized governance, and these values can change quickly with each update of the app. 

To deal with this semi-structured data, Precog turns records into sequences of text-based identifiers, while dropping infrequent symbols, like unique ids. There are many possible ways of learning from these string representations, with different levels of complexity. Nubank decided to represent customers with a bag-of-words of their events’ symbols. 

Precog learns embeddings of the events in a self-supervised manner, using contrastive learning. A set of events from a customer is placed as the anchor, with a single event randomly removed from it to serve as a positive sample while other events that don’t occur for that customer are randomly sampled as negative. Contrastive learning tries to minimize the distance between the anchor and positive samples and at the same time maximize the distance to negative samples. At the end, a vector for each symbol in the vocabulary is produced and we aggregate them to get the customer representation. 

The system's main component is a pipeline to train events/customers embeddings and then incorporate the learned embeddings into downstream models at training and serving time. To train the embeddings, they use Starspace library, which provides a flexible and efficient implementation to learn any entity embeddings. 

To train a downstream model, they take records that are keyed by anonymous customer identifiers and timestamps, together with the labels they want to predict and any other features, and join them with the relevant sets of events. For example, in order to predict the contact reason for a customer support call, they take the events of that customer from the event window preceding the call and the contact reason as classified by agents. 

At run time, an event consumer microservice (implemented in their canonical business service language, Clojure) transforms the raw event data into a string format and stores it in a low-latency, temporary storage (Redis). At serving time, the downstream model microservice (built in Python) retrieves relevant events from the cache, computes embeddings, and uses them as features for a classification model. 

The first application of Precog is in routing of phone calls, through a downstream model that uses the customer embeddings and other features to rank the most likely product that the customer needs help with. If the model is confident enough, the system routes customers directly to specialists. Otherwise, they will talk to generalist agents, who may transfer them to specialists if they can’t handle the problem themselves. 

By adding Precog embeddings, they managed to increase the volume of calls that are correctly routed to specialized agents without need for customer input by more than 50%, compared to the previous model. This resulted in significant decreases in time to solve issues and increases in customer satisfaction. There were improvements for all the products they tested, but they noticed that the largest gains happened in newer products for which they hadn’t implemented traditional features yet. This confirms their hypothesis that they can replace laborious feature engineering with a more scalable approach. 

Another benefit of Precog is the reduction in latency. For traditional features, they had to call multiple REST services and process the results, which resulted in high worst-case latency. With Precog, the time to fetch and process event data is much lower and more predictable, resulting in lower overall serving latency. The freshness of the events (time between event happening and being available to serve) was a concern initially, but in practice they’re seeing them available almost instantly, making this approach very competitive with synchronous REST retrieval.",

The system uses contrastive learning to learn embeddings of events in a self-supervised manner and has resulted in significant decreases in time to solve issues and increases in customer satisfaction.",
"h1": "Improving Customer Service with Precog: A Deep Dive into Nubank's AI-Powered Routing System",
"image_url": ""

{"output": "Here is a summary of the key points from the article: 

1. The article discusses an improved ETA (Estimated Time of Arrival) prediction model for delivery networks, specifically focusing on DoorDash's platform.

2. The new model uses an MLP-gated MoE (Multi-Layer Perceptron - Mixture of Experts) architecture to improve the expressiveness and learn various types of information automatically.

3. The MLP-gated MoE model employs three parallel encoders: a simple encoder, DeepNet encoder, CrossNet encoder, and Transformer encoder.

4. The simple encoder converts the input into a fixed dimension and normalizes feature values. The DeepNet encoder processes inputs through multiple layers, including numerical features, embeddings, and aggregated time series features. The CrossNet encoder models complexities and interdependencies between temporal/spatial/order features.

5. The Transformer encoder leverages the power of self-attention mechanisms to focus on modeling sequential dependencies and relationships.

6. Each encoder processes different input features, leading to comprehensive learning around various aspects of the information. The outputs from all encoders are combined into a single, rich representation, which is then fed into a multi-layer perceptron to generate probabilistic ETA predictions.

7. The new model also incorporates time series features to improve responsiveness to dynamic changes in the system and better adapt to changing conditions over time.", "source": "The article 'Understanding MLP-gated MoE architecture' from DoorDash Engineering Blog"}

"prompt": "You are a model trained to write summaries of articles. Your task is to summarize the article provided and provide a response that includes the key points, findings, and conclusions made in the article. The article below is about using large language models for query understanding in search engines.

The article discusses the use of large language models (LLMs) for improving the precision of query understanding in search engines. It highlights that LLMs can provide a good framework for query understanding, offering deep contextual understanding that other methods may lack. However, it also notes that relying solely on LLMs has drawbacks, such as scalability issues and the need for frequent updates and re-processing. To address these limitations, the article suggests a hybrid approach that combines LLMs with other methods that generalize well to new query-document pairs. The article provides an example of DoorDash's popular dish carousel, which relies on this retrieval pipeline to display relevant results for queries that reflect a specific dish intent. The implementation of the new query understanding and retrieval improvements led to a substantial increase in the trigger rate of popular dish carousels and a more than two percent increase in whole page relevance (WPR) for dish-intent queries, indicating improved user engagement and conversion.

Key points:
1. LLMs can provide deep contextual understanding for query understanding in search engines.
2. A hybrid approach that combines LLMs with other methods is suggested to address scalability issues and the need for frequent updates.
3. The implementation of the new query understanding and retrieval improvements led to improved user engagement and conversion on DoorDash's popular dish carousel."}

{"model_name": "distilbert-base-cased",
"inputs": {
    "text": [
        "At Digits, an ML model is never itself the final product. We strive to delight our customers with well-designed experiences that are tightly integrated with ML models, and only then do we witness the final product. Many additional factors come into play:",
        "Latency vs. Accuracy",
        "A more recent pre-trained model (e.g., BART or T5) could have provided higher model accuracy, but it would have also increased the model latency substantially. Since we are processing millions of transactions daily, it became clear that model latency is critical for us.",
        "Design for false-positive scenarios",
        "There will always be false positives, regardless of how stunning the model accuracy was pre-model deployment. Product design efforts that focus on communicating ML-predicted results to end-users are critical.",
        "Automation of model deployments",
        "The investment in our automated model deployment setup helped us provide model rollback support. All changes to deployed models are version controlled, and deployments are automatically executed from our CI/CD system. This provides a consistent and transparent deployment workflow for our engineering team.",
        "Devise a versioning strategy for release and rollback",
        "To assist smooth model rollout and a holistic quantitative analysis prior to rollout, we deploy two versions of the same ML model and use TFServing’s version labels (e.g., “release” and “pre-release” tags) to differentiate between them. Additionally, we use an active version table that allows for version rollbacks, made as simple as updating a database record.",
        "Assist customers, don’t alienate them",
        "Last but not least, the goal for our ML models should always be to assist our customers in their tasks instead of alienating them. That means our goal is not to replace humans or their functions, but to help our customers with cumbersome tasks. Instead of asking people to extract information manually from every transaction, we’ll assist our customers by pre-filling extracted vendors, but they will always stay in control. If we make a mistake, Digits makes it easy to overwrite our suggestions. In fact, we will learn from our mistakes and update our ML models accordingly."
    ]
},

', '', original_code)

', '', mutant_code)

The article covers topics such as the importance of feedback buttons, tools for prompt engineering, identifying what a good response should look like, carving out uninterrupted periods of time for prompt engineering, soliciting feedback from a small percentage of customers, looping in legal early, and working out if you have easy access to all the data you need.",
"content": "If you’re thinking of building AI into your product, here’s some advice I wish we’d had before embarking on our mission: 

AI should give power to humans, not the other way round 🤖
Feedback buttons are critical ✅
Tools can make prompt engineering much easier 🔧
Identify what a good response should look like 🔍
Carve out uninterrupted periods of time for prompt engineering 👨‍💻
Solicit feedback from a small % of your customer base 📩
Let the rest of your company know if the timelines look different ⏱️
Loop in legal early 👮‍♂️
Work out if you have easy access to all the data you need 🚀
Once you’ve built the first one, subsequent AI features should be easy! ✨

This summary was human-generated."
}

{"role": "assistant", "content": "This is an article about the development of a generative co-pilot application named Boba, which mediates conversations between users and OpenAI's GPT3.5/4. The article discusses several patterns and approaches used to facilitate these interactions, including: Templated Prompts, Structured Responses, Adopting Personas, and Function Calling. Here's a brief summary of each pattern: 

1. **Templated Prompts**: Use a text template to enrich a prompt with context and structure. This helps in producing more relevant and useful completions from the LLM.

2. **Structured Response**: Tell the LLM to respond in a structured data format, such as JSON. This makes it easier for the application to parse the output of the LLM and further operate on its behalf.

3. **Adopting Personas**: Start by telling the LLM to adopt a specific persona, like a visionary futurist, to help generate more relevant responses.

4. **Function Calling**: A new feature from OpenAI that allows developers to describe callable function signatures and their respective schemas as JSON, and have the LLM return a function call with the respective parameters provided in JS."}

raw_response:
{"name": "GraphQL Mock Generation",
"description": "A system that generates realistic mock data for GraphQL queries using Large Language Models (LLMs) and integrates it with existing GraphQL infrastructure.",
"prompt": {
    "GraphQL Mock Generation": [
        "The system takes in a GraphQL query document, along with any associated schema documentation and design URLs. It then generates a prompt for an LLM that includes the necessary context to produce realistic mock data.",
        "The LLM processes the prompt and returns a JSON response containing the mock data. The system validates this data against the GraphQL schema to ensure its accuracy.",
        "If the validation is successful, the system writes the mock data to a file along with a companion source file that provides functions for loading the mock data into application code.",
        "The system also includes an automated check that ensures mock version hashes are up-to-date when code is submitted, ensuring that all generated mocks stay in sync with queries as they evolve over time.",
        "In addition to generating realistic mock data, the system allows client engineers to iterate on features without waiting for the backend server implementation by using a second directive, @respondWithMock."
    ],
    "GraphQL Query Evolution": [
        "The system embeds version hashes in each generated JSON file, allowing it to determine when a given mock needs to be updated based on changes in the GraphQL query document.",
        "When code generation runs, the system determines whether existing mocks’ hashes differ from what their current hashes should be. If they match, it skips mock generation for those types.",
        "If one of the hashes changed, the system intelligently updates that mock by including the existing mock in the context provided to the LLM, along with instructions on how to modify it.",
        "The system avoids unnecessarily modifying existing mock data for fields that are unchanged and still valid, since doing so could overwrite manual tweaks made to the JSON by engineers or break existing tests that rely on this data."
    ],
    "Local Development": [
        "By using @generateMock and @respondWithMock directives, client engineers can significantly speed up their local development and make working with local data more enjoyable.",
        "@respondWithMock allows client engineers to unblock themselves from waiting on the server implementation, since they can easily use locally mocked data when querying unimplemented fields."
    ]
},
"use_cases": [
    "GraphQL Query Testing",
    "Local Development",
    "Feature Iteration without Server Implementation"
]
}

{"ai_platform": "Salesforce", "ai_model": "Text Classifier", "question": "Analyze the text provided and determine if Airtable's embedding system is using eventual consistency. If so, explain how they handle data inconsistencies.", "answer": "Yes, Airtable's embedding system uses eventual consistency. They handle data inconsistencies by tracking the state of each piece of data that needs to be embedded with an 'EmbeddingState'. This state includes a lastPersistedTransaction and lastUpdatedTransaction number. When data changes, the embedding state's lastUpdatedTransaction is updated to reflect the current transaction. The system then generates tasks to re-embed the data if necessary, persists the embeddings in their vector database, and confirms with MemApp that the write has happened, updating the lastPersistedTransaction. If an update has been outpaced, no more work is necessary. This approach allows Airtable to handle various cases such as migrations, failures, and permission changes by resetting the embedding config when embeddings are or about to become invalid."}

raw_response: {"key":"AI_assistant","model":"stuff","website_name":"Stuff","heading":"AI Analysis for the Article 'Engineering a User-Centric Digital Economy: The Future of Recommender Systems'","subheading":"An in-depth analysis of the article by Ben Heymann, discussing the role and future of recommender systems in shaping user behavior and creating a more user-centric digital economy.", "content":"The article 'Engineering a User-Centric Digital Economy: The Future of Recommender Systems' by Ben Heymann discusses the importance of recommender systems in understanding and meeting user needs, thereby shaping a more user-centric digital economy. The author emphasizes that recommender systems are not just about predicting behavior or matching products with users; they are about comprehending the complexity of user decision-making. Here's an analysis of key points from the article:1. Role of Recommender Systems: Heymann highlights that recommender systems play a crucial role in shaping user behavior through informed choices. They help users make decisions by providing them with relevant information and recommendations, thereby reducing friction and improving user experience.2. User-Centric Approach: The author advocates for a more user-centric approach to digital economy, where recommender systems are designed to understand and meet user needs effectively. This includes understanding the complexities of user decision-making, such as browsing and consumption policies, and optimizing utility functions based on these insights.3. Conversational AI: Heymann discusses the potential impact of conversational AI on recommender systems. Chatbots can efficiently gather and present information around shopping options, making it easier for users to make informed decisions.4. Future of Recommender Systems: The author envisions exciting new avenues for recommender systems, including the integration of advanced AI technologies that can transform user interactions and leverage user choice models.5. Ethical Considerations: Heymann acknowledges the ethical implications of recommender systems, such as personalization being tightly related to incrementality, and emphasizes the need for continual innovation to shape a more user-centric digital economy that truly understands and meets user needs.In conclusion, the article by Ben Heymann provides valuable insights into the role and future of recommender systems in shaping a more user-centric digital economy. By understanding the complexities of user decision-making and leveraging advanced AI technologies, recommender systems can help users make informed decisions, reduce friction, and improve overall user experience."}

Your goal is to extract key information from this text.", "completion": "The text describes a machine learning project for generating recommendations on a social media platform called Fishbowl. The project uses embedding methods to generate reasonable recommendations in the absence of labeled data. The content model used is Doc2Vec, which learns semantic similarities between posts. To compute a user embedding, the average post content (text) embedding of all the posts a user liked during the training period is taken. A graph model is also proposed that views Fishbowl as a graph where vertices are defined as unique user ids and post ids, and an edge is defined as an undirected edge between a user id and a post id if the user liked a post. The graph model uses Doc2Vec BFS to generate embeddings for each vertex in the graph (user id or post id). The final hybrid embedding is concatenated from both content and graph models. The project also considers other text features like user title, user company, user location, post feed name, post feed description for personalization. The graph vertex ID embedding methods described assume all post ids or user ids already exist during prediction requests, which makes them not ideal for real-time inference. In the next blog post, they will see how they can circumvent this problem and still utilize a graph structure by using feature information of vertices instead of the vertex id itself for training to generate high-quality embeddings."}

{
"model_name": "journey-aware-notification-system",
"paper_title": "Journey-Aware Notifications: A Personalized Approach to User Engagement",
"abstract": "This paper discusses the development and implementation of a journey-aware notification system at Pinterest. The system aims to generate personalized user journeys based on user data, such as search history, activity history, and boards. It uses hierarchical clustering to form journey clusters and specialized models for journey ranking, naming, and expansion. The system runs on a streaming system, allowing for full inference when there's algorithm change or daily incremental inference for recent active users. The paper also covers the key components of this innovative system, including user journey extraction and clustering, journey naming & expansion, journey ranking & diversification, journey stage prediction, and user journeys output.",
"keywords": ["user engagement", "personalization", "streaming system", "hierarchical clustering", "journey awareness", "LLMs"],
"introduction": "The paper discusses the development and implementation of a journey-aware notification system at Pinterest. The system aims to generate personalized user journeys based on user data, such as search history, activity history, and boards.",
"methodology": "The methodology involves leveraging a rich set of user data, including user search history, user activity history, and user’s boards. User Journey Clustering is performed using hierarchical clustering to form journey clusters. Journey Naming & Expansion, Journey Ranking & Diversification, Journey Stage Prediction, and User Journeys Output are the key components of this innovative system.",
"results": "The initial experiments demonstrate the significant impact of Journey-Aware Notifications: The system drove statistically significant gains in user engagements. Compared to our existing interest-based notifications, journey-aware notifications demonstrated an 88% higher email click rate and a 32% higher push open rate. User surveys revealed a 23% increase in positive feedback rate compared to interest-based notifications.",
"conclusion": "The paper concludes with ongoing efforts to leverage large language models (LLMs) to infer user journeys given user information and activities, offering several key benefits such as simplification, quality improvement, and increased efficiency and scalability."
}

        "extracted_text": "You are a model trained to write in the style of a professional writer and can assist with writing tasks such as summarizing articles, rephrasing sentences, and generating text based on given prompts. Here is your response to the provided article about The Outfitter, an AI-powered process that creates outfits for fashion items.

The Outfitter, which made its debut in the Nordstrom 2021 Anniversary Sale, is an AI-powered process that produces stylist-quality outfits. It operates as a two-phase process: first, it chooses a set of compatible categories based on a statistical, graph-based model called an outfit template; second, for each category in the template, it uses precomputed vision model vectors and an approximate KNN algorithm to choose a list of products that are visually compatible with items already in the outfit. This process is repeated until all categories in the outfit template are filled with specific fashion items.

The Outfitter was evaluated using a stylist survey, where stylists had difficulty discerning outfits created by The Outfitter from those created by human stylists. It also achieved a Top-1 Accuracy of 0.56 and Top-2 Accuracy of 0.80 on the Fill-In-The-Blank (FITB) task, which is a common benchmark in academia for outfit models of this type.

Future exploration includes creating outfits in a specific style, personalizing outfits, removing any dependency on explicit taxonomy, and improving Stylist-Outfitter interaction. The Outfitter has the potential to support capsule wardrobes and deliver personalization at scale to customers."
    }

This process is repeated until all categories in the outfit template are filled with specific fashion items.

{"base":"[{"name":"case","arguments":{}}]","head":["You are a model trained to analyze and summarize text data. Here is an article about using Fourier Transform for predicting defrost periods in refrigeration cases at Walmart.

The article explains that the challenge is to detect defrosts that are periodic in short term but slowly shifting on a longer time frame. To solve this, they use Fourier Transform to process the case temperature signal and extract defrost signal from it. The extracted defrost signal needs to be converted into a binary indicator for downstream modeling.

The model is tested against cases where the defrost command is accurate and readily available in IoT sensor data. The initial results are encouraging, with precision and recall being over 80% on average for cases where defrosts are regular. However, performance is low for cases where defrosts are aperiodic or lack general pattern.

The article also mentions that they will build on top of the current logic using a customized approach based on refrigeration case type, contents, and inputs from domain experts."],"body":["The article explains that to predict defrost periods in refrigeration cases at Walmart, they use Fourier Transform to process the case temperature signal and extract the defrost signal from it. The extracted defrost signal is then converted into a binary indicator for downstream modeling.

To do this, they first convert the case temperature signal from time domain into frequency domain using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). They identify that steady state temperature has zero frequency (dc signal) and highest magnitude, while defrost signal belongs to the frequency with second-highest magnitude. They then filter the frequency spectrum to contain only the frequency belonging to defrost and filter out the rest.

Next, they take Inverse Fourier Transform of the filtered frequency spectrum to get the defrost signal. The extracted defrost signal is converted into a binary indicator by thresholding it at a suitable point and classifying the upper portion as the defrost indicator.

The model is tested against cases where the defrost command is accurate and readily available in IoT sensor data. The initial results are encouraging, with precision and recall being over 80% on average for cases where defrosts are regular. However, performance is low for cases where defrosts are aperiodic or lack general pattern.

The article also mentions that they will build on top of the current logic using a customized approach based on refrigeration case type, contents, and inputs from domain experts."],"tail":["The model allows them to generate defrost commands for cases where the command is not readily available in data, predict future defrost schedules, and identify and correct historical defrost command for cases where defrost command in IoT data is inaccurate.

References are provided at the end of the article."],"id":"5f836e24-170a-4c9d-b84a-88e3e388e63a"}

raw_response: {"ai_platform": "Salesforce", "ai_model": "Mistral", "question": "Analyze the given text and provide a summary of the main points.", "answer": "The text discusses two approaches for improving product categorization in a large dataset. The first approach, referred to as semantic-agnostic, uses OpenAI's CLIP encoders to get embeddings for both product image and title, fuses them with an attention layer, and trains a finetuning network. The second approach, semantic label smoothing, uses CLIP embeddings for product category names to construct soft targets based on the top 20 nearest neighbors of each label. The text presents experimental results showing that the semantic-aware approach outperforms the semantic-agnostic approach in terms of both accuracy and the number of top predictions falling within the top 20 nearest neighbors of the ground truth label. The text also acknowledges the support of several individuals and provides references to related work."}

raw_response:
{
"query": "Google search algorithm",
"answer": {
  "title": "Understanding Google's Search Algorithm: A Deep Dive into RankBrain, Mustang, and Twiddlers",
  "text": [
    "The Google search algorithm is a complex system that uses various components to deliver relevant results for user queries. Here's an overview of some key elements:",
    "",
    "1. **RankBrain**: This artificial intelligence system helps process searches with uncommon or ambiguous terms by learning from past user behavior and refining search results over time.",
    "",
    "2. **Mustang**: A ranking system that generates a list of potential results (the 'green ring') for a given query, which is then filtered down to the final 10 results (the 'blue ring') by Superroot.",
    "",
    "3. **Superroot**: This system performs the precision work of re-ranking the initial 1,000 potential results generated by Mustang, using various filters known as Twiddlers.",
    "",
    "4. **Twiddlers**: These are plug-in-like components that can adjust the ranking position or IR score of search results based on specific criteria such as thematic focus, commercial intent, and more. There are over 100 different types of Twiddlers in use.",
    "",
    "5. **Ascorer**: A component responsible for calculating the Initial Ranking Score (IR) for each document in the index based on factors like keyword relevance and page quality.",
    "",
    "6. **PerDocData**: A database that stores additional information and signals for each document, which can be accessed by various systems to fine-tune relevance.",
    "",
    "7. **QBST**: The system responsible for analyzing user queries and retrieving relevant terms from the inverted index."
  ]
}
}

{"model_name": "Unified Browse pCTR Model",
"description": "A wide-and-deep architecture model that combines deep learning and factorization machine techniques for click-through rate prediction in online advertising.",
"features": [
    {"name": "User Features",
     "description": "Features about the user to whom the ads impressions are presented, including user ID, historical views, clicks, purchase patterns, behavioral signals, and supplementary features."},
    {"name": "Product Features",
     "description": "Attributes pertaining to the product under evaluation, including product ID, name, brand, broad category, price, and specific product details such as whether the item holds attributes like being alcoholic, kosher, or organic."},
    {"name": "Context Features",
     "description": "Features that describe the contextual backdrop against which an ad impression is presented, including types of the placement the ads appear on, and features specific to certain contexts such as reference product attributes on item detail surfaces and collection names on surfaces where collections of products within a store are exhibited."},
    {"name": "Mean Target Encoding (MTE) Features",
     "description": "Features that encode the historical CTRs for specific data segments, which offer valuable insights into past engagements and serve as potent predictors of future performance."},
    {"name": "Data Sparsity and Missing Features",
     "description": "A notable challenge imposed upon working context features is the substantial imputation required within the training data. In our model, we opted for a straightforward approach of imputation with default values."}
],
"architecture": [
    {"name": "Deep Side",
     "description": "Relies on three large-scale embedding matrices that are trained specifically for capturing essential information from features such as product id, user id, and various textual attributes. The embedding vectors are concatenated and fed through multiple fully connected layers."},
    {"name": "Wide Side",
     "description": "Carefully selects a range of features to include low cardinality categorical attributes, especially surface type indicators, as well as continuous features. These wide side features, along with the embedding vectors, are concatenated before we introduce pairwise interactions between these features by employing a classical factorization machine."},
    {"name": "Second-order interaction",
     "description": "Incorporates a basic second-order interaction layer via a factorization machine, primarily for its simplicity of implementation and demonstrated efficacy in boosting the model’s performance."}
],
"performance_metrics": [
    {"name": "Test Log Loss", "improvement": "Not specified"},
    {"name": "Test AUC-ROC", "improvement": "4.8% on Store Root surfaces, 20% on Collection surfaces, and 18% on Other surfaces"},
    {"name": "Test AUC-PR", "improvement": "10% on BIA surface, 48% on Store Root surfaces, and 190% on Collection and other surfaces"},
    {"name": "Calibration", "improvement": "64%-77% improvement over legacy Browse XGBoost pCTR models on all surfaces except BIA"}
],
"impacts": [
    {"description": "Substantially improved incremental sales and return on ads spend (ROAS) for the advertisers"},
    {"description": "Decreased ads end-to-end latency by onboarding to the new model serving"}
]
}

raw_response: {"ai_platform": "Salesforce", "ai_model": "Text Generator", "question": "Analyze and summarize the article 'From Two Sources to One Ranking' about implementing Hybrid Search in a search engine. What are the main steps taken, what were the results of the experiments, and what is the next step for this project?", "answer": "The article discusses the implementation of Hybrid Search, which combines keyword-based retrieval with semantic search, in a search engine. The main steps taken include: 1. Introducing vector search within the extension flow to isolate and measure its standalone impact. Results were overwhelmingly positive, with a more than 100% increase in successful (seller contact) events per user. 2. Launching an A/B test using a linear re-ranking strategy to combine the two retrieval sets while ensuring visibility for vector search ads. The results of this experiment were never expected to be strong in terms of performance, but it succeeded in producing data for retraining the Learning to Rank (LTR) model. 3. Launching an end-goal experiment to validate Hybrid Search in the main search flow with the Vanilla LTR variant. The results showed promising improvements in search efficiency and a reduction in the number of searches per session. 4. Collecting interaction data on vector results for future LTR retraining from another experiment on mobile web (mWeb). The next step for this project is to expand and refine the solution by rolling it out across all markets, starting with the largest market."}

"article_title": "The Impact of a Real Estate Legal AI Agent: Orbital Copilot",
"article_summary": "Orbital Copilot is an innovative AI Agent that is revolutionizing the way real estate legal work is conducted. It can digest hundreds of pages of intricate legal text, handle diverse questions, understand context, gather information, conduct supplementary research, summarize documents, simplify language, reference sources, and provide transparent reasoning. Orbital Copilot's efficiency is a game-changer for lawyers, reducing the time for a comprehensive lease report by up to 70%.",
"keywords": ["Orbital Copilot", "AI Agent", "Real Estate Legal Work", "Document Analysis", "Question Resolution", "Contextual Understanding", "Information Gathering", "Supplementary Research", "Targeted Summarization", "Language Simplification", "Trusted Referencing", "Transparent Reasoning"],
"article_content": "Orbital Copilot, our innovative AI Agent at Orbital, is revolutionizing the way real estate legal work is conducted. It closely emulates the tasks of real estate lawyers, who often wade through extensive legal documents to perform due diligence for their clients. Here’s how Orbital Copilot is transforming the field:

Comprehensive Document Analysis
It can digest hundreds of pages of intricate legal text across numerous PDF documents whether typed, written manuscript or both
Diverse Question Resolution
From straightforward questions like ‘What is the date of the lease?’ to more complex queries such as ‘How has the service charge varied?’, Orbital Copilot handles them all
Contextual Understanding
It tracks down definitions within documents to enhance understanding and reasoning
Thorough Information Gathering
Whether it’s following the breadcrumb trail across one or several documents, it ensures all necessary information is collated
Supplementary Research
It seeks out additional legal information to refine its understanding of the lawyer’s initial instructions. This could be a proprietary legal knowledge base or data from HM Land Registry
Targeted Summarization
Orbital Copilot can summarize entire documents or specific sections across multiple documents
Language Simplification
It adeptly rephrases complex legal jargon into layman’s terms for client comprehensibility
Trusted Referencing
Orbital Copilot indicates the parts of the PDF documents it consulted, facilitating direct navigation to the primary evidence supporting its answers so lawyers can immediately see and trust where an answer came from
Transparent Reasoning
Like consulting a junior lawyer to ask how they came to their conclusion, it transparently reveals its thought process and how it arrived at specific conclusions so a lawyer can focus on the legal nature of the problem and not some ‘black box’ technology
Specifically tailored for real estate legal tasks, Orbital Copilot’s combination of features yields remarkable time savings for lawyers. Considering the busy schedules of legal professionals, often billing in six-minute increments, Orbital Copilot’s efficiency is a game-changer. Our thorough testing with top-tier UK law firms, involving real client work, revealed that Orbital Copilot can reduce the time for a comprehensive lease report by up to 70%. Given that a single property’s lease report can take 2-10+ hours depending on complexity, this translates to substantial financial savings per property for law firms and their clients. Given the regularity of lease reports in real estate law, the cumulative efficiency and cost savings are substantial."

raw_response: {"model": "bert-base-cased", "generated_text": "The model discussed in the article is a machine learning model developed by Canva's engineers for their Draw feature. The model uses a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) to identify shapes drawn by users, and it runs entirely on the client-side, making it possible for the feature to work offline. The model consists of a single Long Short Term Memory (LSTM) layer, followed by a General Matrix Multiply (Gemm) layer. The total number of parameters in the model is approximately 64,109, and it is less than 300 lines long. The model is trained as a multi-class multi-label classifier, using sigmoid activation functions on each output class, and it rejects the prediction if no classes are above a given threshold. The model is used in conjunction with a template-matching approach to accurately align user-drawn paths with vector-graphic representations of shapes."}

raw_response:
{"question":"What are the lessons learned about building an LLM-based product that actually works and ends up in production rather than languishing as a cool tech demo?", "answer":"1. You Do Not (Usually) Need an LLM: The first and most important step is a product one, not a technical one. Before diving into using LLMs, think carefully about whether it's necessary for your product. If you have a problem that benefits from LLMs where you can mitigate the inevitable drawbacks, and you can design the right interface and cost model to make it work, then you can create the future. 

2. Build a Robust Evaluation Framework: Even when you mitigate randomness, you still need to ensure consistent quality of results. You should build a multi-layered evaluation system to manage this. This includes automated validation, expert review by a person, and storing all evaluations to build training data for future improvements.

3. Design the Right Interface: The association between LLMs and chat interfaces is extremely strong; however, it has unfortunately become the standard for most models. Consider other types of solutions that might be more suitable for your use case.

4. Cost Model: As of December 2024, the cost of running a powerful LLM is still a few dollars per million tokens and returns full results in tens of seconds. This is too slow and expensive for many consumer applications. You should design a cost model that works for your specific use case.

5. Continuous Improvement: Regular prompt refinement, based on accumulated feedback, helps continuously enhance the system’s performance. Use templates to re-run your meal generations for new prompts and models easily."}

  "value": "You are asked to analyze a document about personalized search results on an e-commerce platform. The document discusses the methods used for generating personalized search results and their performance. Here is a summary of the key points: 

1. Personalized search results are generated by considering user's historical interactions with the platform, such as clicks, favorites, add-to-carts, and purchases. These interactions are used to create contextual vectors for each user.

2. There are three types of contextual features: Tf-Idf vectors, interaction-based graph embeddings, and item-interaction vectors. Tf-Idf vectors consider listings from a textual standpoint, while interaction-based graph embeddings capture the larger interaction context of query and listing journeys.

3. The personalized search results are ranked using decision trees that take user historical features, similarity measures between users' contextual vectors and candidate listing vectors, and other non-personalized features as input.

4. In online A/B experiments, the personalized model showed an improvement in ranking performance from a purchase’s normalized discounted cumulative gain (NDCG). Users also clicked around less in personalized results, indexed to fewer pages, and bought more items compared to the control model.

5. Serving personalized results online for individual users introduces latency challenges due to the exploding amount of information that needs to be stored.

6. The document also discusses some considerations and challenges related to personalization, such as understanding when to deploy personalized search results, dealing with new users who haven't interacted with many listings, and respecting user privacy choices."
}}

"1":{"name":"text","arguments":{"text":"You are correct. Here is the analysis based on the provided text:

The AI feature was tested in two phases. In the first phase, 75% of the user base was assigned to the treatment group, where they could opt to use the AI feature, while the other 25% formed the control group and were not exposed to the feature. However, the primary success metric, which measures the percentage of activities that get submitted out of the ones that start the product creation wizard, was significantly lower than expected in the treatment group. This was due to user confusion and a lack of trust in the AI tool. The UI didn't explain what the automatic content creator was or what it was helping them do. Additionally, activity providers in the treatment group were spending longer on the pages that were not filled out by AI, as they were frustrated about having to fill out some of the pages manually.

In the second phase, improvements were made to the UI and the LLM to improve the content quality and to automatically fill out more sections. The UI improvements reduced user drop-off from the AI input page to the following page in product creation by 5 percentage points. Users also had similar times spent on non-AI-assisted pages of product creation, so the time on these manual pages was decreased from the first experiment (due to users having been informed what was coming and knowing what they would have to do themselves).

The end result of the second phase was an increase in all success metrics. By assisting activity onboarding, activity providers were more likely to complete onboarding for their activities, and the higher quality content meant we saw a solid increase in activity performance for activities onboarded through AI. The feature was then launched to 100% of the user base.

Key learnings from this project include having perseverance in your convictions, being data-driven, collaborative communication, maintaining central knowledge repositories and singular source of truth databases, early analytics involvement for new product features, expecting the unexpected and paying meticulous attention to detail, outliers are insightful, expectation setting shapes user behavior, anticipating potential issues before they arise, and setting up monitoring systems to track them.

Source: <https://www.getalpine.co/blog/ai-assisted-content-creation>"}}
}

{"model_name":"bart-large","source":"
Grammarly Research: Multilingual Editing with Large Language Models
==============================================================

In this paper, we present mEdIT (Multilingual Editing with Instruction Tuning), a method for training large language models (LLMs) to accept edit instructions in one language and perform those edits in several other languages. We demonstrate that with careful fine-tuning, these LLMs perform much better than all other models built to date, and receive positive feedback from human evaluators.

Our approach involves fine-tuning pre-trained multilingual LLMs on a large dataset of instruction-text pairs in multiple languages. The fine-tuned models are then evaluated on several editing tasks, including grammar correction, simplification, and paraphrasing, across six language families: English, German, Chinese, Spanish, Arabic, Japanese, Korean, and Hindi.

We find that the fine-tuned LLMs generalize well to languages they haven't been specifically fine-tuned for, and perform at a high level no matter what language the instructions are in or whether the language matches the body of the text. We also observe that model size is crucial, with larger models showing significantly improved performance across all tasks.

We make our work and data publicly available on GitHub and Hugging Face to foster further inquiry into the topic. We also encourage interested readers to check out open roles on Grammarly's Strategic Research team if they are passionate about exploring and leveraging the potential of LLMs and generative AI to improve writing and communication for everyone.

**Keywords:** large language models, multilingual editing, instruction tuning, grammar correction, simplification, paraphrasing, fine-tuning, human evaluation", "summary":"This paper presents mEdIT, a method for training large language models to accept edit instructions in one language and perform those edits in several other languages. The approach involves fine-tuning pre-trained multilingual LLMs on a large dataset of instruction-text pairs in multiple languages. The fine-tuned models are then evaluated on several editing tasks, including grammar correction, simplification, and paraphrasing, across six language families.
